EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure over the Tennessee Valley on the morning of the 7th raced rapidly northeastward to the Canadian Maritimes by the morning of the 8th.  The storm brought heavy snow to much of the State.  Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches across all but Rockingham and southern portions of Cheshire and Hillsborough Counties where 3 to 6 inches fell.

An area of low pressure over the Great Lakes on the morning of the 16th slid southeastward to off the New Jersey coast by the morning of the 17th, and then out to sea.  The storm brought 4 to 8 inches of snow to much of southern New Hampshire with the greatest amounts along the seacoast.

The energy from a storm slipping southeast from the Great Lakes merged with the energy from low pressure off the southeast U.S. coast to form an intense area of low pressure off the mid-Atlantic coast by the morning of January 4th.  The intense low brought heavy snow and high winds to much of the region, with blizzard conditions to the Seacoast area.  In addition, the storm brought coastal flooding and erosion along the coast.  The storm brought 10 to 15 inches of snow to much of New Hampshire will lesser amounts along the Connecticut River Valley.

Unusually warm weather combined with 1 to 3 inches of rainfall to produce ice jams on numerous rivers across New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rain produced ice jams and brief minor flooding on the Souhegan River (flood stage 9.0 ft), which crested at 9.38 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unusually warm weather combined with 1 to 3 inches of rainfall to produce ice jams on numerous rivers across New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rain produced brief minor flooding on the Contoocook River (flood stage 5.5 ft), which crested at 5.88 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unusually warm weather combined with 1 to 3 inches of rainfall to produce ice jams on numerous rivers across New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rain produced ice jams and flooding on the Pemigewasset River at Plymouth (flood stage 13.0 ft), which crested at 17.19 ft. Several buildings on Route 175A were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unusually warm weather combined with 1 to 3 inches of rainfall to produce ice jams on numerous rivers across New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rain produced ice jams and minor flooding on the Connecticut River at North Stratford (flood stage 13.0 ft), which crested at 14.84 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed off the Florida coast on the 3rd of January before moving north on the 4th. The intense low (956 mb) passed southeast of Nantucket Island during a period of very high astronomical tides. The forecast astronomical tide at Hampton was 10.5 feet. The storm tide reached 13.3 feet due to an accompanying 2.8 foot storm surge. Flood stage is 11.0 feet. This produced $1 million dollars in damages in coastal Rockingham County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure combined with high astronomical tides to produce coastal flooding in Hampton. Major flooding occurred in Hampton where three feet of water covered some roadways. Ten automobile rescues occurred with up to two feet of water on Ashworth Avenue. Several homes where inundated along the backbay area of Hampton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed off the New Jersey coast (981mb), on March 2nd before rapidly intensifying well offshore on March 3rd (974mb), producing hurricane force wind gusts in the Gulf of Maine. The storm then tracked in a loop and retrograded back towards the southern New England coastline. This occurred during the highest astronomical tide of the month, leading to moderate coastal flooding from Portland through York and Rockingham counties. As the system moved back offshore and became quasi-stationary, large, long period swells reached the coast for several consecutive tide cycles from March 3rd through the 5th and produced major beach erosion, splash-over and coastal inundation in local areas along the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Hampton, New Hampshire, major flooding occurred along the New Hampshire seacoast where up to 3 feet of water covered some roadways in the Back Bay area of Hampton. Route 1A was shut down at Wallis Sands Beach. Ocean Boulevard was also closed in Rye as large rocks, the size of beach balls, were tossed across the road during subsequent high tides. Many of the sea walls along Ocean Boulevard were undermined. The sea wall at North Beach in Hampton was badly compromised.||At Fort Point, water of a half foot to 1 foot deep occurred on Mechanic, Marcy and Ceres Streets in Portsmouth with many properties suffering damage during several tide cycles. The Piscataqua River rose until it reached the decks of water front restaurants. North Mill Pond overflowed onto neighboring streets and properties.

An area of low pressure over the Southeast U.S. on the morning of the 6th moved offshore and began to intensify during the day as it moved slowly northeast to the Virginia Capes by the morning of the 7th.  The storm spread snow across the area during the day of the 7th as it continued to move slowly northeast.  Heavy snow developed overnight with the storm reaching the Gulf of Maine by the morning of the 8th.  Snow eventually tapered off during the afternoon as the storm moved away from the region.||Snowfall amounts ranged from 10 to 18 inches across most of the State with slightly less in the upper Connecticut River Valley.

Low pressure intensified off the mid Atlantic coast (993 mb) on March 7th and moved to just off the northern New England coast by March 8th (989 mb). The low produced storm-force wind gusts over the outer waters. The storm then tracked in a small loop and retrograded back towards the Maine coast. Although this event occurred during a period of low astronomical tides, tides were still running high from a large ocean storm several days earlier. The impact from this system was enhanced because much of the coastal armor such as dune systems and rocky revetments had been compromised or destroyed from the storm earlier in the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ocean storm triggered a storm surge of over two feet at the time of high tide, yet tide levels never reached flood stage. Beach erosion and splash-over were reported in Hampton as 20 foot waves coincided with the time of high tide. This caused damage to area roads. Large stones the size of beach balls were plowed off the road on Ocean Boulevard in Hampton.

Low pressure off the Southeast U.S. coast on the morning of the 12th intensified rapidly as it moved slowly northeast to just southeast of Cape Cod by the morning of the 13th.  The low continued its slow movement to Nova Scotia by the morning of the 14th.  ||The storm brought heavy snow to the almost the entire State with the heaviest snow falling in a strip just inland from the coast.  Snowfall amounts ranged from about 6 inches across Coos County to more than 24 inches  across portions of Hillsborough, Rockingham, Belknap, and Carroll Counties.

Low pressure developed off the New Jersey coast (981mb), on March 2nd before rapidly intensifying well offshore on March 3rd (974mb), producing hurricane force wind gusts in the Gulf of Maine. The storm then tracked in a loop and retrograded back towards the southern New England coastline. This occurred during the highest astronomical tide of the month, leading to moderate coastal flooding from Portland through York and Rockingham counties. As the system moved back offshore and became quasi-stationary, large, long period swells reached the coast for several consecutive tide cycles from March 3rd through the 5th and produced major beach erosion, splash-over and coastal inundation in local areas along the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Hampton, New Hampshire, major flooding occurred along the New Hampshire seacoast where up to 3 feet of water covered some roadways in the Back Bay area of Hampton. Route 1A was shut down at Wallis Sands Beach. Ocean Boulevard was also closed in Rye as large rocks, the size of beach balls, were tossed across the road during subsequent high tides. Many of the sea walls along Ocean Boulevard were undermined. The sea wall at North Beach in Hampton was badly compromised.||At Fort Point, water of a half foot to 1 foot deep occurred on Mechanic, Marcy and Ceres Streets in Portsmouth with many properties suffering damage during several tide cycles. The Piscataqua River rose until it reached the decks of water front restaurants. North Mill Pond overflowed onto neighboring streets and properties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed off the New Jersey coast (981mb), on March 2nd before rapidly intensifying well offshore on March 3rd (974mb), producing hurricane force wind gusts in the Gulf of Maine. The storm then tracked in a loop and retrograded back towards the southern New England coastline. This occurred during the highest astronomical tide of the month, leading to moderate coastal flooding from Portland through York and Rockingham counties. EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Hampton, New Hampshire, the astronomical tide was 10.2 feet on March 2nd. The storm tide reached 12.81 feet from this multi-cycle storm due to an accompanying 2.6  storm surge. Flood stage is 11.0 feet. Major flooding occurred along the New Hampshire seacoast where up to 3 feet of water covered some roadways in the Back Bay area of Hampton. Route 1A was shut down at Wallis Sands Beach. Ocean Boulevard was also closed in Rye as large rocks, the size of beach balls, were tossed across the road during subsequent high tides. Many of the sea walls along Ocean Boulevard were undermined. The sea wall at North Beach in Hampton was badly compromised.||At Fort Point, the storm tide topped out at 12.18 feet on March 2nd. Flood stage is 11.5 feet. Water of a half foot to 1 foot deep occurred on Mechanic, Marcy and Ceres Streets in Portsmouth with many properties suffering damage during several tide cycles. The Piscataqua River rose until it reached the decks of water front restaurants. North Mill Pond overflowed onto neighboring streets and properties.

The melting of the remaining winter snowpack combined with 1 to 2 inches of rain causing minor flooding on the Connecticut River at Dalton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and snow melt combined to produce minor flooding on the Connecticut River at Dalton (flood stage 17.0 ft). Flooding extended into the first two days of May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 1 to 2 inches caused minor flooding on the Suncook River at North Chichester.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 1 to 2 inches produced minor flooding on the Suncook River at North Chichester (flood stage 7.00 ft), which crested at 8.12 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated convection formed ahead of a warm front lifting northeast through the region during the  afternoon of June 30th. An isolated cell in Coos County produced 1.00 inch hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced 0.88 inch Hail near Berlin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated convection formed ahead of a warm front lifting northeast through the region during the  afternoon of June 30th. An isolated cell in Coos County produced 1.00 inch hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1 inch hail in Berlin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong convection developed on a pre-frontal trough ahead of an approaching cold front on the afternoon of June 18th. A deep layer of moderate to strong shear accompanied this boundary as it pushed in from the west. Moderate helicity values resulted in good rotation with some storms. Although two tornadoes were confirmed and other funnel clouds were reported, the majority of the storms produced straight line wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees and wires on Route 112 in Woodstock.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A hiker on Mt. Pemigewasset filmed a small tornado that touched down briefly in a remote wooded area about 3 miles northwest of the Town of Lincoln in Grafton County.  The video showed the small tornado in contact with the ground on the side of a nearby wooded hillside. Rapid rotation was noted in the cloud base.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-0 tornado touched down near Bath in Grafton County and continued east-southeast for about 9.5 miles.  The tornado downed trees along the Ammonoosuc River near the Bath/Haverhill line.  The path of snapped, uprooted, and other downed trees continued to the east southeast, crossing Goose Lane Road and then paralleling State Route 112. The tornado passed very close to the Swiftwater Covered Bridge before continuing east southeast.  Tree damage was also noted northeast of the intersection of State Routes 112 and 116 in the Town of Easton, NH||Given the lack of structural damage and the nature of the tree damage, a rating of EF0 was assigned to this tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado touched down just east of the Vermont border in southwestern New Hampshire during the evening of May 4 and moved rapidly to the east-northeast.  The first observed damage in New Hampshire was in North Walpole in northern Cheshire County. The tornado then moved through the southeastern corner of Charlestown, and the towns of Langdon, Acworth, Lempster, Bradford, Warner, and Webster.  The last observed damage was near Route 127 in Webster.  Total track length was about 38.5 miles with a maximum width of about 300 yards.  The tornado was rated E-F1 based on the damage in the Town of Warner where winds were estimated to have gusted to 90 to 100 mph. ||This was the 2nd longest tornado recorded in New Hampshire history and also the 2nd earliest tornado in New Hampshire since records started in 1950.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down in northern sections of North Walpole and moved rapidly to the east-northeast.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado that touched down in northern North Walpole raced across southern Sullivan county at about 60 mph.  The tornado crossed through Charlestown, Langdon, Acworth, Lempster, and Washington before heading into Merrimack County.||The girls softball team at Fall Mountain Regional High School was trapped for about an hour when large trees blocked the access road to the school.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong  cold front was approaching from the west on the afternoon of May 4th. Good shear combined with strong forcing to produce organized convection in the form of bowing line segments and some super cells. Thunderstorm wind damage was widespread with these storms as they moved through New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed a tree on Cunnington Lane in Colebrook.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong  cold front was approaching from the west on the afternoon of May 4th. Good shear combined with strong forcing to produce organized convection in the form of bowing line segments and some super cells. Thunderstorm wind damage was widespread with these storms as they moved through New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed a tree on Mountain Road in Raymond.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved off the South Carolina coast on the morning of the 17th, then travelled rapidly northeast to the Gulf of Maine by the morning of the 18th.  The low then moved rapidly away from the area.  The low brought a heavy snowfall to the southeastern part of the State with snowfall amounts generally ranging from 6 to 9 inches. The northern and western part of the State generally saw 2 to 6 inches of snow.

An EF-1 tornado touched down just east of the Vermont border in southwestern New Hampshire during the evening of May 4 and moved rapidly to the east-northeast.  The first observed damage in New Hampshire was in North Walpole in northern Cheshire County. The tornado then moved through the southeastern corner of Charlestown, and the towns of Langdon, Acworth, Lempster, Bradford, Warner, and Webster.  The last observed damage was near Route 127 in Webster.  Total track length was about 38.5 miles with a maximum width of about 300 yards.  The tornado was rated E-F1 based on the damage in the Town of Warner where winds were estimated to have gusted to 90 to 100 mph. ||This was the 2nd longest tornado recorded in New Hampshire history and also the 2nd earliest tornado in New Hampshire since records started in 1950.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado that touched down in northern North Walpole raced east-northeast at about 60 mph across southern Sullivan County and into Merrimack County.  The tornado crossed through Bradford and Warner with intermittent damage observed before lifting off in the Town of Webster.  The most severe damage was observed in the Town of Warner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moisture-laden area of low pressure produced 4 to 6 inches of rain in 6 hours in southern New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A moisture-laden area of low pressure produced 4 to 6 inches of rain in 6 hours flooding several streets in Milford. Wall, Alder, and Crosby Streets flooded. Mason Rd. near the Wilton town line was severed after a culvert washed away. Ox Brook came out of its bank flooding Melendy Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front dropping south from Canada produced scattered convection as it moved into northern New Hampshire on the afternoon of September 3rd. A few cells became severe and produced wind damage in Lancaster and Jefferson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees, wires and power poles on Route 135 in Lancaster.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A moisture-laden area of low pressure produced 4 to 6 inches of rain in 6 hours resulting in flooding of 4 businesses on Tinkham Avenue in Derry. Seven people were rescued by boat and 15 cars were submerged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moisture-laden area of low pressure produced 4 to 6 inches of rain in 6 hours in southern New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A moisture-laden area of low pressure produced 4 to 6 inches of rain in 6 hours causing the Souhegan River at Milford to flood briefly.  The river crested at 9.17 feet.  Flood stage is 9 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving up the Appalachian Mountains produced 1.5 to 3 inches of rain across New Hampshire and western Maine. This resulted in minor flooding due to an ice jam on the Pemigewasset River at Woodstock and minor flooding at Plymouth.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving up the east coast produced 1.5 to 3 inches of rain across New Hampshire resulting in an ice jam and minor flooding on the Pemigewasset River at Woodstock (flood stage 9.00 ft), which crested at 9.83 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of thunderstorms produced 2.5 to 4 inches of rain in less than 3 hours which caused flooding and road washouts in portions of Hillsborough County. Cavendar, Kimble, Middle, Tannery Hill Road, and 19 Mile Roads in Hancock were washed out. The intersection of Forest Road and Peterborough Street was also washed out.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced 2.5 to 4 inches of rain in less than 3 hours in Hancock resulting in many road washouts. Cavendar, Kimble, Middle, and Tannery Hill Roads were washed out. The intersection of Forest Road and Peterborough Street was also washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of thunderstorms produced 1.5 to 2.5 inches of rain in less than 3 hours in Hanover New Hampshire. Route 10 at Pinneo Hill Road was closed due to flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced 1.5 to 2.5 inches of rain in less than 3 hours in Hanover which caused flooding and the closure of Route 10 at the intersection with Pinneo Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very hot and humid air mass was in place over the region on the afternoon of August 7th with temperatures in the 90s and dew points in the lower 70s producing CAPE values up to 3000 J/KG. An upper trough approaching from the west brought increasing shear into the region as storms began developing in the afternoon. Multiple reports of wind damage were associated with these storms and one injury occurred due to falling tree limbs and debris.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees and utility poles in Swanzey.

In Hooksett, White Hall and Harvest Drive were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approaching from the northwest was the focus for late afternoon and evening convection on the 15th of August. Moderate shear with the associated shortwave increased the damaging wind potential. An isolated cell in central New Hampshire became severe and caused a couple reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees and wires on Route 3 and Parker Road in Carroll.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was draped across central New Hampshire into the foothills of western Maine on the morning of August 3rd. South of this boundary, a warm and very humid air mass was in place and convection began developing by late morning in the unstable warm sector. Due to the high precipitable water values, several of these storms produced wet micro bursts and damaging winds along with a few reports of hail. Some isolated flash flooding also occurred with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees and wires  in South Sutton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was lifting north through the Connecticut Valley on the afternoon of August 17th. Clearing in the warm sector allowed for moderate CAPE values by late in the afternoon across southern New Hampshire. Several of the storms that developed produced wind damage during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed a tree on power lines on Flat Roof Mill Road in Swanzey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two people died and 6 others had to be rescued when a strong rip current developed along the beach in Seabrook.   A 49-year-old man was taken to a nearby hospital and pronounced dead shortly after the incident.  His 47-year-old wife, who was also taken to the hospital, died the following day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man and woman from Massachusetts died and 6 others had to be rescued when a strong rip current developed along the beach in Seabrook, New Hampshire.   Both victims were administered CPR at the scene and taken to a nearby hospital.  The  49-year-old man was pronounced dead shortly after arriving at the hospital.  His 47-year-old wife died the following day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak low pressure system dove across Manitoba October 22nd and skirted the northern Great Lakes before redeveloping in the Gulf of Maine and Canadian Maritimes early on October 24th. Precipitation along the north of the low track changed over to snow, and fell heavily at times in the mountains of northern New Hampshire and nearby western Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain showers across southern Coos County in the early afternoon gradually became steady through late afternoon and began mixing with and changing to snow. In the higher elevations, precipitation began as snow at the onset. Snow fell heavily at times during the evening, especially above 1500 feet elevation. As low pressure moved northeast, the precipitation transitioned to upslope snow showers and continued to add to the snowfall totals. Mount Washington received over 20 inches of snow, while closer to 1500 feet 12 inches of snow fell northwest of Randolph. In the valleys, 1 to 3 inches of snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving up the east coast produced 1 to 2 inches of rain across southern New Hampshire. Rivers were already high from previous rainfall events and the additional rainfall resulted in minor flooding on the Suncook River at North Chichester.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced 1 to 2 inches of rainfall across southern New Hampshire resulting in minor flooding on the Suncook River at North Chichester (flood Stage 7.00 ft), which crested at 7.80 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked along a stalled frontal boundary draped across southern New England and brought widespread snowfall to the area. Late on the 19th and early on the 20th very light rain and snow persisted along the stalled front. As low pressure approached on the morning of the 20th precipitation gradually changed to all snow and became steadier. Snow continued through the day on the 20th as temperatures hovered near freezing. Low pressure continued to strengthen in the Gulf of Maine and helped to prolong the snow into the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Early on the morning of the 20th a parallel mesoscale band of snow developed over central New Hampshire, just to the north of the mid level warm front. Snow persisted under this feature into the mid afternoon, where a narrow swath received 6 to 8 inches of snow. As temperatures aloft cooled in the mid-afternoon, the snow to liquid ratios became very high for the last 1 to 2 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This winter storm began as a blizzard over the Midwest. The storm moved into the eastern Great Lakes early on the 26th and snow broke out over northern New England that afternoon and evening. Secondary low pressure developed in the vicinity of Cape Cod on the evening of the 26th helping to ensure cooler air remained trapped across much of New Hampshire. Surface temperatures hovered within a couple degrees of freezing, but aloft temperatures were much colder. As a result snowfall was heavy and wet, and very dependent on elevation. While lower elevations mixed at times with sleet, freezing rain, and rain, the higher terrain remained predominantly snow and significant accumulations occurred. Low pressure slowed to a crawl on the 27th and snow continued into the 28th, although the bulk of the accumulation was from the evening of the 26th to the afternoon of the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Steady snow began on the evening of the 26th. Southeast flow downsloping off the White Mountains delayed significant snowfall accumulation for much of the early part of the event. Light accumulations occurred through the morning of the 27th. Once flow became westerly and upslope enhanced snowfall began accumulations began to pile up quickly north of the notches. Total snowfall ranged from 6 to 12 inches, though additional accumulation continued in upslope snow showers into the 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This winter storm began as a blizzard over the Midwest. The storm moved into the eastern Great Lakes early on the 26th and snow broke out over northern New England that afternoon and evening. Secondary low pressure developed in the vicinity of Cape Cod on the evening of the 26th helping to ensure cooler air remained trapped across much of New Hampshire. Surface temperatures hovered within a couple degrees of freezing, but aloft temperatures were much colder. As a result snowfall was heavy and wet, and very dependent on elevation. While lower elevations mixed at times with sleet, freezing rain, and rain, the higher terrain remained predominantly snow and significant accumulations occurred. Low pressure slowed to a crawl on the 27th and snow continued into the 28th, although the bulk of the accumulation was from the evening of the 26th to the afternoon of the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Steady snow began late on the evening of the 26th. Orographic lift of southeast flow into the White Mountains allowed snow to be heavy at times early on the 27th. Significant accumulations occurred in the higher terrain and south facing slopes, while much lighter accumulations occurred near the Connecticut River. Once flow became westerly and upslope enhanced snowfall began accumulations near the river began to catch up to those in the mountains. Total snowfall ranged from 6 inches to a foot or more in the higher elevations, though additional accumulation continued in upslope snow showers into the 28th.

Significant accumulations occurred in the higher terrain and south facing slopes, while much lighter accumulations occurred near the Connecticut River. Once flow became westerly and upslope enhanced snowfall began accumulations near the river began to catch up to those in the mountains.  Surface temperatures near freezing made for a heavy, wet snow and scattered power outages due to downed tree limbs. Total snowfall ranged from 6 inches to a foot or more in the higher elevations, though additional accumulation continued in upslope snow showers into the 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified as it moved up the east coast to near Cape Cod, Massachusetts on November 27th. This allowed for gale force northeasterly winds to generate nearshore waves of 10 to 15 feet and increased storm surge values. This coastal flood event coincided with a period of high astronomical tides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure combined with high astronomical tides to produce coastal flooding in Hampton. Six inches of water covered roads in the Backbay area while significant splash-over occurred along Route 1A in Hampton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This winter storm began as a blizzard over the Midwest. The storm moved into the eastern Great Lakes early on the 26th and snow broke out over northern New England that afternoon and evening. Secondary low pressure developed in the vicinity of Cape Cod on the evening of the 26th helping to ensure cooler air remained trapped across much of New Hampshire. Surface temperatures hovered within a couple degrees of freezing, but aloft temperatures were much colder. As a result snowfall was heavy and wet, and very dependent on elevation. While lower elevations mixed at times with sleet, freezing rain, and rain, the higher terrain remained predominantly snow and significant accumulations occurred. Low pressure slowed to a crawl on the 27th and snow continued into the 28th, although the bulk of the accumulation was from the evening of the 26th to the afternoon of the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Steady snow began on the evening of the 26th. Orographic lift of southeast flow into the White Mountains allowed snow to be heavy at times early on the 27th. Significant accumulations occurred in the higher terrain and south facing slopes, while lighter accumulations occurred at lower elevations. Surface temperatures near freezing made for a heavy, wet snow and scattered power outages due to downed tree limbs. Total snowfall ranged from 6 inches to a foot or more in the higher elevations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Coastal low pressure developing off the Mid Atlantic spread snow across the Northeast on the evening of November 15th. While the strongest lift remained to the south, moderate to heavy snow did spread into parts of the area, especially southern New Hampshire. This was due mainly to strong warm air advection, as a strong jet streak rounded the base of the upper level low over the Ohio River Valley. This jet streak lifting northeastward over the region allowed the dry slot to move into the area after midnight, bringing steady snowfall to an end.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the late evening of the 15th a band of heavy snow lifted out of southern New England into southern New Hampshire. Snowfall quickly increased in intensity, and rates of 1 inch per hour were observed after midnight. Early morning of the 16th a dry slot moved overhead and precipitation intensity dropped off and at times mixed with sleet or rain. As low pressure strengthened in the Gulf of Maine one final band of snow developed and quickly moved northeast across the area, leaving behind light accumulations. Total snow accumulations were generally in the 6 to 8 inch range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly developing low pressure formed off the Mid Atlantic coast and tracked through southern New England. Forcing resulted from an open wave aloft, which limited intensity and duration of precipitation. Despite a track near the coast, a seasonably cold antecedent air mass allowed precipitation to begin as snow before mixing with and changing to rain at the coast into the Lakes Region. Precipitation stayed mainly snow in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow overspread the North Country of New Hampshire early on the morning of the 13th. Snow gradually increased in intensity, becoming moderate for a stretch from near sunrise to mid-morning. In the early afternoon temperatures began to warm and snow mixed with rain at times at lower elevations, before coming to an end altogether.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving through New England produced 2 to 4 inches of rain in southern New Hampshire. This caused minor flooding on the Suncook River at North Chichester and on the Warner River at Davisville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced 2 to 4 inches of rainfall across southern New Hampshire resulting in minor flooding on the Suncook River (flood stage 7.00 ft), which crested at 7.92 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching shortwave and increasing shear combined with a slow moving cold front to produce scattered showers and thunderstorms on the afternoon of July 28th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1.25 inch hail in Bow Lake Village.
